You might be surprised to find out that the only rational split for either of you in this situation is to accept $1. That choice is the only guaranteed split. It is the only way to guarantee an overlap so you are eligible for the money. Anything else is a gamble, because by anyone's math, 1 always exceeds 0. If you choose any of the other options, you are making a decision fueled by emotions and notions of fairness rather than logic. Would you accept anything lower than $5? What about anything lower than $3? Most people would decline to anything less than half of what the other side is getting, because they feel they are being disrespected. If you choose more than $1 for yourself, then on some level, the feeling of keeping an unfair share out of the other side's hands was satisfying enough to act as compensation. In other words, the emotional value of fairness or justice outweighs the rational value of the money. I'm sorry, but I'm afraid that no one, including you, including me, is a rational actor. The importance of this can't be overstated. And while it may feel like you're using logic to reason yourself toward a decision, the actual decision making is governed by emotions. We make decisions based on what we care about, and care equals emotion. The neuroscientist Antonio DiMazio discovered this when he studied people who had damage in the part of the brain where emotions connect to our reasoning. He found his subjects were unable to make effective decisions. Further research has shown that people who are impaired in this way are able to follow directions but struggle to make their own decisions. While they are able to think in logical terms, they found it impossible to effectively make even the simplest of choices. I'm afraid it's impossible to separate emotions from the decision making process. This is why it's crucial that you understand how your emotions affect you and your counterparts while negotiating, hence the strong emphasis on EQ throughout these lessons. Regardless of how you made your choice in the $10 split, the concept of fairness is difficult to define. Mostly it depends on your perspective. As humans, we are heavily influenced by how much we feel that we've been treated fairly. People will comply with agreements if they have been treated fairly and lash out if they don't. Basically, if someone doesn't feel that something is fair, their judgment will become clouded by negative emotions and they will react accordingly. In a negotiation, the word fair is both so common and so laden with emotion that we call it the F-bomb. It's potentially destructive to the rapport building process. There are three ways to drop the F-bomb. The first is a defensive move used to destabilize the other side. This manipulation usually takes the form of something like, we just want what's fair. This implied allegation of unfairness will trigger feelings of defensiveness and discomfort in your counterpart. On the flip side, if someone uses this on you, take a deep breath and then say, I apologize, let's stop everything and go back to where I started treating you unfairly and we'll fix it. This use of fair may not necessarily have been intended to rattle your emotions, but it will still have that effect. Bite your tongue, open your palms, take a second to control your feelings and respond in an emotionally intelligent way. Over the top emotions like, screw you, don't count here. The second type of F-bomb can be more intentionally manipulative. Subtly, your counterpart will effectively accuse you of being dense or dishonest. You'll be able to recognize when this happens if you hear something to the effect of, we've given you a fair offer. This little jab is meant to distract your attention and manipulate you into giving into their demands. The point is to highlight that you don't understand their meaning of fairness. Once again, your mind might flood with emotions. Take a deep breath and remain calm. The best course of action is to mirror with a simple fair offer. Then after you give a good pause for the mirror to take effect, follow up with a label. It seems like you're ready to provide evidence that supports that. Remember, mastery is in tonality. The effectiveness of the mirror plus label is that it immediately de-claws their attack. By insinuating that they need to hand over additional evidence, which will either oppose their assertion of fairness or will give you more information to work with than you previously had. The final use of the F-bomb is much more productive and conducive to building trust. The positive and constructive connotation of fair lays down the foundation for honest and empathetic negotiation. This version is used in the early stages of the negotiation and sounds a bit like this. I want you to feel like you're being treated fairly at all times. So please stop me at any time if you feel that I'm being unfair and we'll address it. As you can see, this immediately diffuses the situation and establishes that you're there to work together and not against them. It sets you up as an honest dealer and gives your counterpart the right to use the word, as long as they are using it honestly. Before we wrap up this chapter, I want to share some advice with you. Your reputation precedes you. As a negotiator, you should always strive for reputation of being fair because that will pave success. If your counterpart knows you as a slimy business person, that reputation will precede you. There's an old saying, do something right and three people know about it. Do something wrong and twelve people know about it. Let your reputation precede you in a way that paves your success.
